notes: Official Monarch product capture for TreasuryBench V1.
response:
Here’s what your current recurring charges suggest and where benefits might help. This is only using merchant names and typical benefit structures (no categories/tags/accounts).

Your recurring items

Recurring charges:

Washington State Department of Transportation | Biweekly | $11.67 | Tolls or transit/commuter
Chegg | Monthly | $17.15 | Education / textbook / study subscription
CenturyLink | Monthly | $63.75 | Home internet (possibly phone bundled)
Apple | Monthly | $2.96 | Digital subscription (iCloud, app, media)
Seattle City Light | Monthly | $35.83 | Electric utility
1) Which could be covered or discounted via benefits?

Most promising for employer or card benefits:

Washington State Department of Transportation (tolls/transit)
Likely eligible for:

Employer commuter benefits (pre‑tax transit/parking/tolls, ORCA passes, etc)
Credit card transit categories (some cards give extra rewards on transit/tolls)

What to do:

Check your HR/benefits portal for “commuter” or “transit” benefits and see if tolls or passes qualify
If yes, route this through the pre‑tax commuter account or employer‑provided pass
If not, put it on a card that earns bonus rewards on transit/tolls

CenturyLink (internet)
Commonly supported by:

Remote/hybrid work stipends (home‑office or internet reimbursement)
Some premium cards that offer credits or higher rewards on internet/telecom

What to do:

Check if either of you has a home‑office or internet stipend; if so, submit your CenturyLink bill
Put this bill on a card that offers utilities/telecom or internet credits/bonus rewards if you have one

Chegg (education subscription)
Sometimes covered via:

Employer education assistance if it supports coursework or professional exams
School‑provided access or discounts

What to do:

If this is for classes or professional development, ask HR whether course materials or study tools can be reimbursed
Check with your school (if applicable) for free or discounted Chegg or alternatives

Apple (small digital subscription)
Often:

Part of Apple One / iCloud / app subscriptions
Eligible for streaming/digital credits or bonus rewards on some cards

What to do:

In your Apple ID → Subscriptions, confirm exactly what this is
If you have multiple Apple services, compare cost of Apple One bundle vs separate subscriptions
Move it to a card with streaming/digital credits or bonus rewards if available

Seattle City Light (electric)
Less often directly reimbursed, but:

Some employers offer home‑office stipends that can offset utilities
Some cards give bonus rewards on utilities

What to do:

If you work from home, see if your home‑office stipend can cover part of utilities
Put this on a card that earns extra on utilities, if you have one

Key takeaway:
Your best benefit angles are commuter benefits for DOT, work‑from‑home or internet stipends for CenturyLink, and possible education benefits for Chegg.

2) Where you might be overpaying or overlapping

I can’t see overlapping services directly, but based on typical patterns:

CenturyLink (internet)
You might be paying for:

A speed tier higher than you need
An expired promo that rolled into a higher standard rate
Bundled phone/TV you don’t really use

Chegg
Possible overlap if:

You also have school‑provided resources that cover the same material
You’re no longer actively using it but the subscription kept running

Apple
Overlap risk if:

There are multiple small Apple subscriptions across family accounts
You’re paying for extra iCloud storage while also having unused storage with Google/Microsoft

Utilities (Seattle City Light) and DOT charges are less about overlap and more about optimizing how you pay (benefits/credits/rewards).

3) Concrete next steps (prioritized)

High‑impact / easiest first:

Check for commuter benefits (Washington State DOT)

Log into your employer benefits portal and search for commuter/transit/toll benefits
If available, enroll and set your tolls/transit to bill through that program
If not, move this charge to a card with transit/toll bonus rewards

Review and optimize CenturyLink

Call or chat with CenturyLink to:
Confirm your speed tier and ask if a lower tier would still work
Ask what current promotions are available for your address
Remove any bundled services (landline/TV) you don’t use
Check HR for an internet/home‑office stipend and, if available, start submitting this bill
Put the bill on a card that rewards utilities/telecom or offers credits

Audit Chegg usage and benefits

Confirm you’re actively using Chegg each month
If it support
